title: Diagnosing a hen not laying
subtitle: Working through the common reasons a laying hen stops producing eggs.
excerpt: A healthy hen in her first two years should lay most days when the light is long. When she stops, the cause is almost always one of a handful of common things: moult, broody behaviour, nutritional gap, or a physical issue. This is a systematic approach to telling them apart.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Welfare Code of Practice for Laying Hens (2018). BHWT henkeeper resources. Smallholder Magazine, laying and health features.

content.text: A hen that has stopped laying is not necessarily ill. In most cases the cause is one of five common and easily remedied conditions. Work through them in order rather than jumping to a health concern first.

Laying hens need 14 to 16 hours of light per day to maintain production. In the UK, natural daylight drops below this threshold from October through February. A hen stopping in October or November, especially in her second year or older, is almost certainly responding to the shortened day rather than to any health problem. Adding supplemental light in the coop (a low-wattage bulb on a timer, adding artificial light from 4 a.m. to extend the morning) restores production within two to three weeks.

has patchy feather loss, especially around the neck, back, and wing edges. The coop floor will have more feathers than usual. Moulting hens divert protein to feather regrowth and laying stops or drops sharply. No intervention is needed: once the new plumage is complete (six to eight weeks), laying resumes.

hen sits tight on the nest, clucks in a low continuous tone when touched, pecks if you reach under her, and has a bare warm patch on her belly. She will not lay until the broody cycle is broken. Remove her from the nest box every morning and block access to it during the day. A wire-floored broody coop (three to five days) breaks the hormonal cycle more reliably than blocking alone.

A laying hen needs a minimum of 120 g of complete layer pellets per day plus unlimited water. A bird on scratch grain only, or one being pushed off the feeder by dominant flock members, will reduce or stop laying. Confirm that each bird is getting regular access to feed. If the pecking order is preventing access, add a second feeder on the opposite side of the run.
and run a basic examination. Feel the keel for muscle loss. Check the vent for fouling or prolapse. Feel the crop the morning after fasting: a full hard crop that has not emptied overnight is
and needs treatment. A hen that is eating well but has a distended abdomen (swollen belly that feels fluid-filled) may have internal laying or egg peritonitis, which is a vet visit.
